title: Alabama Blasts Past UCLA in Round 1 of the College World Series
description: After falling behind by two runs, Crimson Tide homers and solid pitching advances Bama to the winners bracket.
fullText:
Jocelyn Briski started off strong by striking out two and coercing a pop out on a third batter (though it sure looked like she had strike three on her as well).

Jena Young and Brooke Wells led off the bottom of the 1st with singles. After two outs, Marlie Giles came through with an RBI single for an early lead.

.@AlabamaSB is fired up for the early lead 😤#WCWS x 🎥 ESPN pic.twitter.com/6hWMRm7Q7c

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 28, 2026

Two more Bruins whiffed in the 2nd. With one out in the 3rd, a Bruin broke up the no-hitter with a liner down into the left field corner. The next batter hit a sky-high pop-up that caused catcher Giles and first baseman Wells to collide, giving the Tide first baseman a bloody lip. After a delay to attend to her, Briski was suddenly struggling with her control. The Tide ace would give up back-to-back homers to UCLA to make it 3-1 Bruins. But then just like that, Briski got it back and continued to mow down the competition.

Bama left Young stranded on third base in the 3rd frame and put two on base in the 4th but could do nothing with it. However, they were hitting the ball pretty hard.

Young reached base for the third straight time in the 5th on a walk. Who else but Alexis Pupillo got Bama right back in the game? She smacked an offering over the right field wall to tie it up.

Pupillo with the POWER 💪 TIE BALL GAME #WCWS x 🎥 ESPN / @AlabamaSB pic.twitter.com/PtZCu0wOF6

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 29, 2026

With two outs in that same inning, Giles singled and Audrey Vandagriff doubled. Ambrey Taylor scorched a ball but unfortunately right at the left fielder.

In the 6th, Kristen White beat out an infield slap single and Young collected her third hit of the game. Wells walked to the plate and stroked a three run homer to put the Tide up by three.

BROOKE WELLS WITH THE 3-RUN BOMB 💣#WCWS x 🎥 ESPN / @AlabamaSB pic.twitter.com/9V6DJRgGrF

— NCAA Softball (@NCAASoftball) May 29, 2026

Briski (24-3) had that one bad span but bounced back in a big way with nine strikeouts against the nation’s most potent offense.

Early in the game on at least two plate appearances, Alabama hitters were clearly instructed to take a pitch on 3-0, only to watch the offering go right down the middle. This Bama team is not like others. It would be preferable if Patrick Murphy trusted his hitters and let them swing away. For the record, the Crimson Tide hitters amassed only two walks.

Alabama had two occasions where fielders collided trying to catch a fly ball. This deep into the season, communications should not be an issue. This is first day stuff. Someone needs to take charge and fielders need to yell out their “I got it”! The announcers tried to excuse it away due to noise in the stadium, but this game was no louder than games at Rhoads or on the road against most SEC teams.

While the noise level from the UCLA dugout seemed high, the Bama bench seemed awful quiet for awhile. Pupillo’s homer sent a jolt through the team and woke everyone up.

Similar to the Super Regional, Alabama had more success at the plate when they swung early in the count.

Briski hit a batter in the 7th, but we never saw a good replay. It sure didn’t look like the batter made an attempt to get out of the way.

Abby Duchscherer, Larissa Preuitt, and Lauren Johnson were late game defensive replacements.

As a team, the Tide has 99 home runs this season, putting the team five shy of tying the single season record of 104 set in 2010.

If Wikipedia is not lying and you include his one season as head coach at Northwest Missouri State, this win puts Patrick Murphy at 1,400 career victories.

WELLS: 2-4, 1 RUN, 3 RBI, hit the ball hard in one out as well.

PUP: 1-4, 1 RUN, HR, 2 RBI and for waking up the team.

YOUNG: 3-3, 3 RUNS, 1 BB, on-base all four plate appearances, a perfect game from a leadoff hitter.

BRISKI: 7.0 Inn, 6 hits and 3 runs allowed, 9 K, 0 BB, WIN

title: Where Matt Campbell lands on CBS Sports’ list of top Power 4 coaches
description: CBS Sports released a list of the top-25 Power Four coaches in college football, with Penn State HC Matt Campbell finishing behind James Franklin.
fullText:
Penn State football has produced a long line of successful coaches over the past 60 years. James Franklin was given the honor of leading the program before his firing last season, and now Matt Campbell is getting that same opportunity. Although the two men likely don’t have much of a relationship, their names will be forever tethered because of the controversy surrounding the coaching change.

Campbell had unheralded success at Iowa State before coming over to Penn State, leading the Cyclones to as many winning seasons in 10 years as they had in the 30 years combined before he took over as head coach. Franklin’s time with the Nittany Lions was ultimately connected to his inability to get them close to a national championship, but he flaunted regular season dominance that the team hadn’t experienced in decades.

They are great coaches who both unsurprisingly found themselves on CBS Sports’ list of the top-25 Power Four coaches. There is sure to be some clamoring over Campbell’s placement at No. 16, which is three spots behind Franklin at No. 13.

The rankings are somewhat understandable considering that Penn State’s new coach has never worked for such a big school with this many expectations. He overachieved at Iowa State, but it will be a whole new ball game with the Nittany Lions, as Campbell will have to face much better competition and more pressure.

While you can certainly make the argument that he’s a better coach than Franklin, CBS Sports gives the nod to the latter based on his experience and career success. Campbell has already achieved

title: SEC presidents, chancellors release statement opposing pooling media rights
description: The SEC opposes part of a new proposed bipartisan bill that would allow conferences to pool media rights for college sports broadcasts.
fullText:
MIRAMAR BEACH − As the SEC concluded its spring meetings on May 28 at the Sandestin Resort, presidents and chancellors released a joint statement opposing the pooling of media rights.

The Cruz-Cantwell bill −a bipartisan bill presented by Sens. Ted Cruz (R, Texas) and Maria Cantwell (D, Washington) − includes a provision that would rework the Sports Broadcasting Act to allow conferences to pool their TV rights. Specifically, conferences can pool rights up to 75% of the FBS membership, which will increase to 138 teams in 2026 with the addition of Sacramento State and North Dakota State.

The SEC entered a landmark 10-year, $3 billion media rights agreement with Disney and ESPN which started in 2024-25. The deal makes ABC and ESPN the exclusive broadcast homes for SEC football and men's basketball.

"The Southeastern Conference recognizes and appreciates the many ongoing discussions regarding potential, system-wide improvements to ensure the future success of college athletics," the statement read.

"The SEC has been intentional, through years of thoughtful planning and decision-making, in strategically positioning itself for future media negotiations. The Conference must retain the ability to act in the best interests of its membership. As such, the SEC does not support assigning the media rights to a third party and remains firmly committed to independently conducting its own media negotiations."

SEC commissioner Greg Sankey said he finished reading the 111-page bill on the night of May 27, which includes limiting student-athletes to one unrestricted transfer over five years while prohibiting midseason coaching movement.

"We have folks working and analyzing things," Sankey said. "The top line questions will be: Does the bill provide us with the necessary protections? ...Figure out what it means for college athletics broadly and what it means for the Southeastern Conference in particular."

Kevin Brockway is The Gainesville Sun’s Florida beat writer. Contact him at kbrockway@gannett.com. Follow him on X @KevinBrockwayG1.

This article originally appeared on The Gainesville Sun: SEC opposes part of new bill that would pool media rights

title: Is Auburn bound for Omaha? Experts weigh in before regionals begin
description: Auburn baseball begins their quest for their seventh College World Series this week, and they have many qualities to get them there.
fullText:
Auburn baseball performed well this season, winning 38 games against the nation's toughest schedule and winning 26 of its 39 games against Quad 1 and Quad 2 competition. Most of their success can be attributed to their pitching staff, which features two 100-plus strikeout throwers and a quality bullpen known for limiting runs late in games.

Auburn has many strong qualities that will get them to Omaha, but what is the likelihood that they will actually reach their ultimate goal? Experts at D1Baseball are high on the Tigers, with six of nine writers on the D1Baseball Regionals Roundtable picking Auburn to appear in their seventh College World Series, and its third since 2019.

The experts at D1Baseball say the best pitching rotation, best bullpen, and best defense all belong to Auburn in the upcoming Auburn Regional, three aspects that the Tigers will definitely use to their advantage. However, several members of the D1Baseball Regionals Roundtable believe Auburn has an easy journey to the top, especially in the regional round.

"The Tigers got a No. 2 seed that we projected as a 3 (UCF), a No. 3 seed that has dealt with major pitching injuries and should have been one of the last four teams in the field (NC State), and a No. 4 seed with a losing overall record and an RPI in the 230s (Milwaukee). This feels like a considerably friendlier draw than the other top-eight seeds received, particularly No. 5 North Carolina, whose 4-seed (VCU) has a No. 84 RPI, and whose 2 and 3 seeds are also formidable," writes Aaron Fitt, who listed Auburn's draw as the softest of the regional round.

Not everything surrounding Auburn is doom and gloom, however. Kendall Rogers not only has Auburn in the College World Series, but projects the Tigers to win the whole thing. He says he hopped on Auburn's hype train during the Tigers' weekend series at Texas A&M earlier this month.

"I wrote about this in College Station a few weeks ago, but goodness, I just love the vibe with this team right now. The Tigers have a potent offensive lineup that is hitting for more power, and it’s hard to find many rotations you believe in more than the Tigers," Rogers writes.

Auburn baseball's journey to Omaha and the College World Series begins Friday at noon CT against the Milwaukee Panthers, winners of the Horizon League Tournament. Check out the full schedule for this weekend's Auburn Regional here.

title: In Hollywood’s spotlight, No. 1 UCLA baseball is stealing the show
description: UCLA has proved it's the best team in the country. Now it relies on its talent and magic to take the Bruins to the College World Series.
fullText:
LOS ANGELES – There’s a long belief if you want to be a star, you go to Hollywood.

The stage is there, it’s all about rising to the occasion.

That’s what is going on with UCLA baseball, which is producing something special — and making sure everyone knows it.

“This team loves the spotlight,” coach John Savage said ahead of Friday's NCAA regional opener vs. Saint Mary's.

FEELING THE HEAT: 5 teams under the most pressure in NCAA tournament

LEFT OUT: Mercer exclusion from NCAA tournament highlights committee's mid-major problem

You don’t need to go to Dodger Stadium to see some baseball excellence in Los Angeles. Instead, you can go up Interstate 405 and make your way to Jackie Robinson Stadium, where one of the most impressive seasons of recent time has put UCLA on a path toward the College World Series.

The campaign has been anything but linear. It has star power, but instead of dominating on a nightly basis, the Bruins are on the stage finding all sorts of ways to pull the rabbit out of the hat. There’s been key injuries, while consistently having to play from behind.

But when you think the act is over, UCLA does it again, offering a new trick to leave everyone — and opponents — stunned. How to explain it?

On that stage, it’s simply Bruin Magic.

There were high expectations for UCLA, starting the season No. 2 in the preseason USA TODAY Sports Baseball Coaches Poll. Doubters questioned the ranking, even more when UCLA went 2-1 against UC San Diego in the opening series.

The questions didn’t last long. The Bruins won their next four to become No. 1 in the country on March 2, and never let go of the top spot.

After a 9-2 start, UCLA won 27 straight games, flirting with the all-time win streak record of 34 while separating itself as the best team in the country. It finished the season as the Big Ten regular season and tournament champions en route to a 51-6 record, the most wins for a team entering the NCAA Tournament since Tennessee in 2022. UCLA also became the first team to go wire-to-wire at No. 1 in the D1Baseball Top 25 poll.

“We're headed in a good direction,” said junior left fielder Dean West. “This season's super fun.”

The Bruins got to the mark with one of the best pitching staffs in the country, with a 3.31 team ERA that is second in the country while also top five in WHIP (1.18, 3rd) and hits per nine innings (7.24, 5th). That’s thanks to having one of the best starters in Logan Reddemann and best closers in the game in Easton Hawk. It sure makes it challenging to beat when the offense averages over eight runs a game.

The Big Ten isn’t a baseball gauntlet, and UCLA hasn’t played a super tough schedule, but whether it was nonconference or in the league, the Bruins stepped up when the lights were brightest. UCLA went 13-1 against ranked opponents, and 11-2 against teams that made the NCAA Tournament.

Good teams need to beat good teams, and it helps when you have a really good — perhaps great — player.

“I think it's a (Roch) Chowlosky factor,” Savage said. “I really do.”

MLB scouts and executives know all about Chowlosky, the shortstop a cut above the rest in his third season in Westwood, continuing to build upon being a national player of the year campaign in 2025.

He has a .329 batting average, .459 on-base percentage, 1.123 OPS and 21 home runs. His defense is exceptional, leading to back-to-back Big Ten player of the year awards and a good chance he is the national player of the year again.

All of the numbers make it easy to see why he’s the heavy favorite to be the top selection in the 2026 MLB Draft. As for UCLA, it’s the intangibles paving the way for success.

Whether it’s firing up the pitcher or everyone in the dugout, taking some major league at-bats to jumpstart a rally, it’s quite the advantage UCLA has.

It’s exactly what’s needed to push through the postseason: Make big-time plays in big-time games.

“When you're talking about premier games, that guy is going to be there in premier games. He just brings everybody, I think, to a different level of mentality and a different level of preparation,” Savage said. “You don't want to play different per se regardless of who you play, that's not a good trait. But you want to make sure that you got your ankles taped and you're ready to go against certain people.”

You can’t deny the record, but boy was it a journey to get there. This team sure does find itself in holes often, but almost every single time, UCLA finds ways to thwart upsets.

Of the 57 games, opposing teams have scored first in 25 of them. When that happens, the Bruins typically come back to win with a 22-3 in such instances.

When opponents score in the first inning? UCLA is 16-0 when allowing a run in the opening frame.

And just when others survive and head into the late innings still ahead, it still isn’t safe. The Bruins are 9-4 when they are trailing after six innings.

It’s what makes this season spectacular. Savage’s club refuses to go down, pulling out trick after trick to end up on top. Just look at their last slate of games.

After Oregon led 6-1 going into the sixth inning with a chance to take the series on May 10, West capped off a comeback with an eighth-inning grand slam.

Washington had the same opportunity in the final series of the regular season, with UCLA down to its final strike. A Cashel Dugger single up the middle gave the Bruins the lead and eventually took the series.

Then there was the Big Ten tournament, with UCLA winning all three games in the final at-bat. A comeback against Purdue capped by sacrifice fly, a walk-off homer off rival USC and a bases loaded hit by pitch against Oregon in extra innings to win the conference crown.

Whether you view it as luck or clutch, just when you think it’s run out, the Bruins keep pulling wins out. They love playing in crunch time.

“That’s what we've been doing all year,” said junior right fielder Payton Brennan. “7-8-9, we talk about a lot like those are our innings. Whatever happened previously, we try to win one inning at a time.”

Not an ideal life to live, but Savage doesn’t really mind it. He said he’s “always looking for adversity” for his team, getting them ready for the postseason.

There is something ominous about being the overall seed in the NCAA tournament. Only two have won it all — Miami in 1999 and Tennessee in 2024 — and of the past six tournaments, the No. 1 overall team failed to reach the College World Series four times.

One of those was UCLA which hasn’t had any luck in the spot, failing to reach Omaha in both instances. If there was an opportunity to deny the Bruins, it would be in the regional rounds.

For as good UCLA has been this season, they have had some struggles against unranked foes, and at home, exactly what they’ll deal with. Five of the six losses came to unranked teams, and five losses came at home, giving Virginia Tech, Cal Poly and Saint Mary’s a great opportunity to upset.

UCLA is also hoping to get Reddemann back, the ace who is 8-0 with a 2.87 ERA in 10 starts, but has been out for over a month with arm fatigue.

But if the Bruins are able to get through Los Angeles, watch out. It’s been mentioned they’ve done great against good teams, but they win on the road, 24-1 away from Jackie Robinson Stadium, a trait Savage mentioned is a strong indicator for championship potential.

It helps they won the Big Ten tournament at Omaha's Charles Schwab Field, getting familiar with winning in the stadium that will crown a national champion. UCLA is trying to cap off one of the best seasons of college baseball with its second title in program history.

“We like to turn it on for everybody, but moving forward, we're only going to play good teams,” Brennan said. “Hopefully, we can keep it going.”

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: UCLA baseball can get Hollywood ending with College World Series run

title: NCAA baseball tournament predictions: Picking every regional winner
description: USA TODAY Sports predicts the winner of every NCAA baseball tournament regional and who keeps hope alive for College World Series trip to Omaha.
fullText:
The college baseball field is set, as 64 teams embark upon the road to Omaha.

Seven of the 16 regional sites of the NCAA Baseball Tournament belong to the SEC, which has housed the past six College World Series winners. However, it isn't until the No. 3 seed that the conference is represented by conference winner Georgia, with UCLA and Georgia Tech taking the Nos. 1 and 2 spots, respectively.

NCAA REGIONALS: 5 teams under the most pressure in baseball tournament

TOUGH BREAK: Mercer exclusion from tournament highlights committee's mid-major problem

Even without tournament mainstays LSU and Vanderbilt, there is a bevy of talent. Projected No. 1 MLB Draft pick Roch Cholowsky is an elite prospect for the top-ranked Bruins, whereas the Yellow Jackets have two projected top-10 picks in Vahn Lackey and Drew Burress. Looking outside of the power conferences, Jackson Flora of UC Santa Barbara will be must-watch when he takes the mound in Austin. And Justin Lebron looks to end his Alabama career with a bang.

Who will make it out of regionals, and who will be upended before getting to host a super regional? USA TODAY Sports predicts the winner of every regional and what that would mean for their super regional matchup.

Teams: No. 1 UCLA, Virginia Tech, Cal Poly, Saint Mary's

The Bruins got the favorable draw you would expect for a No. 1 seed. Virginia Tech looked like a bubble team this season, and Cal Poly might actually be the team most capable of giving UCLA a scare. However, Roch Cholowsky and the Bruins get through a weak field relatively unscathed.

Teams: No. 16 West Virginia, Wake Forest, Kentucky, Binghamton

Wake Forest is probably the best No. 2 seed in the field, and though the Demon Deacons have struggled against quality opposition (5-15 vs Quad 1 this season), they have the firepower at the plate and the pitching to make it through to supers.

Teams: No. 2 Georgia Tech, Oklahoma, The Citadel, UIC

Georgia Tech has been so good this season, and its staunchest test here is an Oklahoma team that is entering the tournament freezing cold. Lackey and Burress lead the Ramblin' Wreck to supers.

Teams: No. 15 Kansas, Arkansas, Missouri State, Northeastern

This shouldn't feel like such an upset pick, but Arkansas is a popular choice to make it out of this gauntlet. Even though Northeastern is probably the best No. 4 seed in the field, Arkansas needs to beat a tough Missouri State team before it even thinks about Kansas. The Jayhawks continue a historic season, but pick up some war wounds along the way.

Teams: No. 3 Georgia, Boston College, Liberty, Long Island

This one isn't really close. Georgia cleans house.

Teams: No. 14 Mississippi State, Cincinnati, Louisiana, Lipscomb

This is another one where the field may cause some bumps and bruises but won't take down the host. Cincinnati has a young and aggressive order but can't run with the Bulldogs for nine innings, and Louisiana is a high-end mid-major that ultimately doesn't have pop up and down the order to keep up with the top two seeds in this region.

More: Is Ryan McPherson difference between Mississippi State early exit or CWS run?

Teams: No. 4 Auburn, UCF, NC State, Milwaukee

This is a weird field. No. 2 is a high seeding for UCF, and it's debatable if NC State should be here at all. Although the Wolfpack have the emotional boost of a retiring Elliott Avent, it's hard to figure anyone really challenging the Tigers at home.

Teams: No. 13 Nebraska, Mississippi, Arizona State, South Dakota State

Absolute, yikes. Nebraska gets the hell group, with two legitimate contenders in Mississippi and Arizona State. No disrespect to the Cornhuskers, who 100% deserve their hosting bid, but the first No. 2 vs No. 3 game here might decide the bracket. Mississippi rides its arms to a bracket win, but there are three serious threats to make it out of this one.

More: How Ole Miss' Cade Townsend turned into top MLB prospect with 30-year-old tennis book

Teams: No. 5 North Carolina, Tennessee, East Carolina, VCU

North Carolina left the ACC title game with a lot to clean up, with catcher Macon Winslow constantly having to deal with spiked breaking balls that ultimately put Georgia Tech out of reach. But even with a tough region, Tennessee is more bark than bite this year, while East Carolina is down Sean Jenkins, Colby Weber and Gavin Van Kempen. VCU is probably the best No. 4 seed in the field, but the Tar Heels survive a brutal group thanks to experience and pitching depth.

Teams: No. 12 Texas A&M, USC, Texas State, Lamar

USC on paper looks like a real threat to make it out of this one, but it's hard to overstate how bad the Trojans have been against equal or better competition. They haven't beaten good teams all year, it's hard to figure they will now. Texas State replaces UTSA as the non-power conference team you don't want to see, but this under-seeded Aggies team has the bats to survive the region.

Teams: No. 6 Texas, UC Santa Barbara, Tarleton State, Holy Cross

It's hard to overstate how desperately we'd like to go against the grain and pick UCSB, but with the Gauchos effectively riding one arm in a double elimination tournament, Jackson Flora just isn't enough. Texas makes it out of the region to host a super regional, but quite possibly drops a game to the top pitcher in the country in the process.

Teams: No. 11 Oregon, Oregon State, Washington State, Yale

Honestly? Shoutout to the committee for a truly hilarious regional. Oregon is probably over-seeded as a No. 11, but it is rewarded with an Oregon State team with a compelling No. 1 seed argument itself. Even without Dax Whitney, Oregon State can get out of the Pac-12 (and Yale) redux region.

Teams: No. 7 Alabama, Oklahoma State, USC Upstate, Alabama State

This region entirely comes down to whether the Crimson Tide can stymie Oklahoma State's bats. If this turns into a slugfest it could get away from 'Bama very quickly. Ultimately, however, Alabama keeps Oklahoma State off the board enough to survive a tough No. 2 seed.

'Earned the right': Alabama coach Rob Vaughn praises Alabama State

Teams: No. 10 Florida State, Coastal Carolina, Northern Illinois, St. John's

Admittedly, Coastal Carolina is a bit of a name pick, but the Chanticleers have a habit of playing above themselves in the tournament. It will be curious to see how they manage Cameron Flukey. If things line up for him to face the Noles one time, all it takes is for Coastal Carolina to steal one beyond that.

More: Link Jarrett 'proud' of FSU baseball hosting third straight regional

Teams: No. 8 Florida, Miami, Troy, Rider

This is a favorable field for Florida. Troy and Rider are kind of write-offs, with the former losing 29 games and getting in as an at-large and the latter being an auto-bid that was fortunate to get in. But Miami could pose some problems for the Gators, albeit not quite enough to steal the region.

Teams: No. 9 Southern Miss, Virginia, Jacksonville State, Little Rock

Jacksonville State is the best No. 3 seed we've seen in some time, and is a popular pick to make it out of here. Part of the reason that's so trendy is because it's easy to sleep on Southern Miss. That is an error in judgement. The Golden Eagles have repeatedly stumbled in the postseason, but make it out of their host region this year.

∎ Kevin Skiver, USA TODAY: Georgia Tech

Georgia Tech hasn't made the College World Series in 20 years. Indeed, it hasn't won its regional in 20 years. It doesn't have any titles.

Who cares, who cares, and who cares. The Yellow Jackets have two possible top-10 picks in Vahn Lackey and Drew Burress, and have often looked the part of the best team in baseball. The big question mark is the pitching. Mason Patel has been able to rise to the occasion as a reliever, while Jackson Blakely has been a workhorse starter. The road to Omaha isn't easy, but Georgia Tech finally has the firepower to get there.

∎ Austin Curtright, USA TODAY: Georgia TechGeorgia Tech took down North Carolina handily 13-6 in the ACC championship game, and I don't think it'll stop there. The Yellow Jackets have real shot at the College World Series, with a nation-leading 22 Quad 1 wins.The lineup also has serious thump. Vahn Lackey and Drew Burress are both projected top-10 picks in the 2026 MLB Draft, and Jarren Advincula (batting .434 this season) could also sneak into the first round. Georgia Tech's pitching still has to prove it in the postseason, but anything is possible with a lineup like that.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: College World Series bracket: NCAA baseball tournament regional predictions

title: Why Kentucky coach Will Stein favors 24-team College Football Playoff
description: Kentucky football coach Will Stein would like the College Football Playoff to feature 24 teams, he told The Courier Journal at SEC spring meetings.
fullText:
MIRAMAR BEACH, FL — If Kentucky football coach Will Stein has it his way, the College Football Playoff will expand to 24 teams, not 16 as championed by SEC commissioner Greg Sankey.

"Anything more than 12 is going to be beneficial to our entire league," Stein told The Courier Journal at SEC spring meetings. "When you go to a nine-game conference schedule, you add a game, so that's eight more losses for the league. You have to add more games. So, it's going to happen. It's just a matter of, is it going to be 16 or 24?"

Stein knows it's not up to him. Or any of the other league's coaches. Sankey and Big Ten commissioner Tony Petitti need to kumbaya their way to a resolution. ESPN executives need to be in on it too. Athletics directors and university presidents matter as well. There are a lot of stakeholders involved in this conversation.

But, as Stein said, CFP expansion is imminent.

At their spring meetings earlier this month, ACC commissioner Jim Phillips and Big 12 commissioner Brett Yormark said they support a 24-team playoff. This would create 13 more games than the current 12-team model, generating more overall revenue and allowing for more access into college football's national championship event. However, Phillips said ESPN "has been pretty clear with all of us that they'd like it to stay at 12, maybe 14, but no higher than 16" teams.

On3 reported earlier this week that a majority of the SEC's athletics directors (nine of the 14 that responded to On3's poll on the subject) prefer a playoff format beyond 16. One AD voted for a 20-team playoff, while eight others opted for 24.

Sankey wants 16 teams, while Petitti wants 24. Both commissioners, On3 reported, would rather continue with 12 than acquiesce to the other's preference. If they don't come to an agreement by Dec. 1, the 2027 playoff will stay at 12 teams. Sankey reaffirmed the league's 16-team predilection Wednesday, adding he expects a formal decision sometime this fall.

With expansion, though, comes the surefire elimination of conference championship games. ESPN reported that the SEC championship game generates an estimated $80-100 million in revenue for the league. Losing that kind of cash flow would be tough, especially with all the financial pressures schools face to remain competitive amid the NIL, revenue-sharing era of college athletics.

"Teams just aren't gonna be willing to play that (game)," Stein said, "and that weekend's gonna be now for playoffs, or Army-Navy, or however they want to decide how that fits. It's inevitable if you add more teams to the playoff, you're going to have to remove something, and it's probably going to most likely be conference championships."

Last year at SEC spring meetings, then-Kentucky football coach Mark Stoops told The Courier Journal he and UK had "a lot of work to do" to be relevant in CFP discussions. Stein, who coached in the last two playoffs with Oregon, is set on transforming UK into a contender. Regardless, the size and makeup of the CFP field will have a trickle-down effect on the Wildcats, as more SEC representation and more SEC wins means more SEC revenue distributions.

Last year, the SEC earned $37.4 million for CFP and bowl game participation. The league, according to Front Office Sports, distributes that money in a hybrid model, benefitting schools in the playoff more than the league's remaining membership.

First-round teams each get $3 million, and those who have to travel get an allowance to do so as "determined by the SEC Executive Committee." Quarterfinal teams get $3.5 million. Semifinal teams get $3.75 million. And a team participating in the national championship gets $4 million. Leftover money is added to the conference's pool and distributed equally among every SEC program and the league's office.

With months left to make an official decision, a lot of the conversations around the CFP at SEC spring meetings have been about what a CFP team should look like. What criteria matters most to the selection committee? Which statistics hold the most weight? What exactly does strength of schedule mean, i.e., do losses matter more than wins?

CFP executive director Rich Clark gave a presentation at the Hilton Sandestin Tuesday on the selection process and various metrics used throughout it. When asked whether that presentation offered satisfactory answers as far as what constitutes a playoff team, Sankey said "yes and no." Stein told The Courier Journal the presentation sparked "good back-and-forth and good discussion."

Reach college sports enterprise reporter Payton Titus at ptitus@gannett.com and follow her on X @petitus25. 

This article originally appeared on Louisville Courier Journal: College Football Playoff size, why Kentucky coach Will Stein wants 24

title: Eastern Michigan’s Magical NCAA Tournament Run Ends Against Stanford
description: Eastern Michigan women’s golf stuns the college golf world with a Cinderella run to the NCAA Final Four, upsetting powerhouse Texas before falling to Stanford.
fullText:
Most Cinderella stories belong to basketball, but this year’s breakout underdog came from the golf course. Eastern Michigan University’s women’s golf team shocked the college golf world by advancing to the Final Four of the NCAA Division I Women’s Golf Championship.

To reach the semifinals, Eastern Michigan upset Texas, whose senior Farah O’Keefe had captured the individual national championship the day before after shooting 12-under par to win by three strokes.

“I told them at lunch, 99 times out of 100, they beat us,” head coach Josh Brewer said. “But why can’t today be that one time?”

Just two years ago, Eastern Michigan’s women’s golf program was ranked No. 226 nationally before Brewer arrived in Ypsilanti. His hiring coincided with a major investment from GameAbove and donor Keith Stone, whose contributions helped fund the university’s new $8 million golf practice facility — a critical tool in Brewer’s recruiting efforts.

In the national semifinals, Eastern Michigan faced powerhouse Stanford, a program loaded with talent. Stanford featured five players ranked 17th or higher in the World Amateur Golf Rankings, while Eastern Michigan’s top player, Janae Leovao, entered ranked No. 143.

“You fear a great player who has nothing to lose,” Stanford coach Anne Walker said earlier this spring. “As long as we continue to dominate the regular season, we’re going to face teams swinging free with nothing to lose, and we’ll have to be at our very best.”

For much of the front nine Tuesday afternoon, Eastern Michigan held its own against the juggernaut Cardinal. Eventually, Stanford’s depth proved too much, as the Cardinal swept all five matches for the second straight round. Stanford star Megha Ganne later said the team’s goal was to finish the week with a perfect 15-0 match-play record.

“Maybe the best team I’ve ever seen. I guess the dream had to end eventually, but I’m just so happy with our team; they battled, and I probably have never had a team excited to accept a semifinalist trophy, but it just shows how far we’ve come in two years,” Brewer said.

Eastern Michigan’s roster reflected both talent and unlikely opportunity. The Leovao twins arrived from Long Beach State with reported five-figure NIL deals, while Savannah De Bock — who followed Brewer from Georgia — was once considered a top-35 amateur prospect.

But beyond those standouts, the Eagles’ run became even more improbable. Sophomore Erina Tan entered the tournament ranked No. 1,697 in the world, while junior Baiyok Sukterm’s path to Eastern Michigan included stops at junior colleges in Kansas and Kentucky before arriving in Ypsilanti. In the semifinals, Sukterm faced Ganne, a U.S. Women’s Amateur champion and one of college golf’s elite players.

Eastern Michigan’s season ended against Stanford, but the Eagles proved they belonged among the nation’s best.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

title: NBA Draft deadline tracker 2026: Latest news on Koa Peat, Tyler Tanner, other notable college stars
description: Here's who's staying in the NBA Draft and who's returning to school next season.
fullText:
NBA Draft deadline tracker 2026: Latest news on Koa Peat, Tyler Tanner, other notable college stars originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

In the NIL era, one of the biggest days on the college basketball calendar is the NBA Draft withdrawal deadline.

Potential first-round picks Labaron Philon, Alex Condon and Tahaad Pettiford were among the players to pull out of the NBA Draft and return to school ahead of last year's deadline, with Philon making a last-minute reversal to stick with the Crimson Tide.

Some potential first-rounders bypassed the NBA Draft altogether this year, including UConn's Braylon Mullins and Florida's Thomas Haugh. Other college stars still have a decision to make ahead of the deadline, set for Wednesday at 11:59 p.m. ET.

The Sporting News is tracking which players are in, out or undecided on the NBA Draft. Follow along below with the latest news.

MORE:Post-lottery NBA mock draft

Here are the notable players with decisions still to be made about the NBA Draft:

Jeremy Fears Jr., Michigan State

Tounde Yessoufou, Baylor (in transfer portal)

Meleek Thomas, Jeremy Fears Jr. and Tounde Yessoufou are the three notable decisions still on the board.

Thomas could join returnee Billy Richmond in heading back to Arkansas, while most have assumed Fears was most likely to return to Michigan State after leading the Spartans' backcourt last season. Yessoufou was reportedly expected to stay in the draft, but more recent speculation has indicated a return might be the most likely scenario.

BIG BOARD: Ranking the top 60 players in 2026 NBA Draft

Former Iowa State star Milan Momcilovic withdrew from the NBA Draft hours before the deadline. Momcilovic entered the transfer portal in April and is set to be the subject of a bidding war, with St. John's, Kentucky, Louisville and Arizona reportedly expected to be in the mix. Momcilovic averaged 16.9 points per game in his junior season at Iowa State, shooting a pristine 48.7 percent from 3-point range.

Vanderbilt star Tyler Tanner is returning to school for his junior season. Tanner was one of the nation's top breakout players last season, averaging 19.5 points, 3.6 rebounds and 5.1 assists for Vanderbilt while shooting better than 48 percent from the floor. After a long wait, the Commodores will enter 2026-27 with their star back in the fold as they try to finish near the top of the SEC again.

After starting in 19 games for the Razorbacks as a sophomore, averaging 11.2 points and 4.3 rebounds per game, 6-6 guard Billy Richmond was initially reported to be staying in the NBA Draft, but further reports indicated he plans to return to Arkansas. Richmond was a projected second-rounder who will bring 3-point shooting to the Razorbacks in 2026-27.

Andrej Stojakovic landed at Illinois in the transfer portal from Cal last offseason, and he proved to be a key acquisition, averaging 13.5 points and 4.5 rebounds per game as the Illini made a Final Four run. He's now set to run it back at Illinois next season after initially entering the draft; five of Illinois' top-eight scorers are slated to return next year.

After two seasons with the Kansas Jayhawks and transferring to Louisville this offseason, Flory Bidunga is heading back to collegiate hoops. The 6-foot-10 forward/center wanted to give it a run on his new squad. Through 35 games last season, Bidunga averaged 13.3 points, 9 rebounds, and 2.6 blocks with the Jayhawks. After a Round of 32 appearance this season, the Cardinals will likely enjoy the big man's presence in the paint.

Former NC State guard Matt Able told Jon Rothstein that he would be returning back to college hoops after one season, and heading 25 miles down the road to join the North Carolina Tar Heels. This season with the Wolfpack, Able averaged 8.8 points, 3.4 rebounds, and just under an assist, while shooting 35.5% from beyond the arc. He will be looking for an influx of appearances on a more noteworthy program.

Freshman center Malachi Moreno, a Kentucky native, was one of the bright spots for Mark Pope's Wildcats in a challenging season and chose to pass on a potential first-round selection to continue his college career. Moreno averaged 7.8 points, 6.3 rebounds and 1.5 blocks per game as a freshman, starting 30 of 36 games. With a quiet offseason for Kentucky and their two biggest additions coming in the backcourt, Moreno could have a major role in the frontcourt in his sophomore season.

Rueben Chinyelu announced his return for a third season at Florida and fourth at the college level, giving the Gators their entire starting frontcourt back for another season. A national champion in 2025, Chinyelu averaged 10.9 points and 11.2 rebounds per game across 35 games last season. Chinyelu will play alongside Thomas Haugh and Alex Condon once again, likely setting up Florida as the preseason No. 1 team.

Former Wisconsin guard John Blackwell was always likely to stay in school after transferring to Duke, and he made it official about a week before the deadline. Blackwell was one of the top scorers on the transfer market after averaging 19.1 points and 5.1 rebounds in his third and final season with the Badgers, shooting 38.9 percent from 3-point range.

Acaden Lewis opted for the portal after a solid freshman season at Villanova, and Miami was always a more likely destination than the NBA right now. Lewis averaged 12.2 points, 5.3 assists and 1.9 steals per game as the Wildcats' point guard, shooting 45.6 percent from the floor despite struggles from beyond the arc.

Elliot Cadeau opted to go through the draft process, but a return to Michigan felt like a formality and became official a week before the deadline. The national champion point guard isn't without limitations, but he proved to be the right fit for Dusty May's squad and averaged 10.5 points, 2.7 rebounds and 5.9 assists per game on his way to a title.

Former Wake Forest star Juke Harris pulled out of the NBA Draft after transferring to Tennessee. Harris averaged 21.4 points and 6.5 rebounds per game as a sophomore, morphing from a role player into an ACC star, and he's part of a loaded Volunteers transfer class for next season.

After a serious car accident and a meniscus injury, Alijah Arenas wasn't sure he would even play at all in his freshman season at USC, but he showed enough after returning to put himself on the NBA radar again. A return to school was ultimately the path Arenas chose after averaging 14.1 points, 2.9 rebounds and 2.1 assists per game while battling efficiency issues on a depleted squad.

After being an All-SEC Freshman Team selection in 2025-26, Amari Allen initially entered his name into the NBA Draft and competed at the Combine in Chicago. But on Wednesday, he announced he would instead be returning to play for the Crimson Tide after starting in 24 of his 32 games last season, averaging 11.4 points, 6.9 rebounds, 3.1 assists, 1.0 steals and 0.7 blocks per game. The 6-8 forward now becomes a key returner for Alabama's 2026-27 unit.

Here are the notable players who decided to stay in the draft; elite prospects such as AJ Dybantsa, Darryn Peterson and Cameron Boozer and more aren't included because there was never any question about their decisions.

Despite speculation Arizona freshman Koa Peat could return after seeing his stock slip, the forward is keeping his name in the NBA Draft. Peat averaged 14.1 points, 5.6 rebounds and 2.6 assist on his way to a Final Four appearance in his freshman season, battling midseason struggles but finishing stronger. Peat will hope he can still sneak into the lottery and looks like a surefire first-round pick regardless.

After two seasons with the Red Raiders, former guard Christian Anderson is keeping his name in the NBA Draft pool. Recent murmurs from the Combine & pre-draft workouts forecast Anderson’s status to remain as a projected top 15-20 pick. Across all 33 starts last season for Texas Tech, Anderson averaged 18.5 points, 7.4 assists (T-4 in the nation), 3.6 rebounds, and shot 38% from beyond the arc.

Former Santa Clara forward Allen Graves told DraftExpress he will be staying in the NBA Draft. Graves was the Broncos' third-leading scorer this season (11.8 PPG) and helped lead the team to its first NCAA Tournament in 30 years. Graves is widely touted as a projected top-20 pick and won ample recognitions for his 2026 campaign, such as WCC Freshman, Sixth Man, and Defensive Player of the Year honors.

Ebuka Okorie made Stanford happy when he said his options were either a return to school or the NBA, bypassing the transfer portal, but the star freshman said at the combine that he expected to stay in the draft. Okorie averaged 23.2 points, 3.6 rebounds and 3.6 assists as a freshman, shooting 35.4 percent from 3-point range and notching 1.6 steals per game. He projects as a mid-to-late first-round pick.

Cameron Carr was one of the breakout stars in college basketball last season, emerging as a Big 12 stud after failing to find a role at Tennessee. The guard averaged 18.9 points and 5.8 rebounds per game and capitalized on his success by declaring for the NBA Draft with reportedly no consideration of returning to school.

Duke has assembled another loaded, deep roster for next season, and it became clear as the Blue Devils made their moves that they knew Isaiah Evans would not be back next season. He later made it official. Evans averaged 15.0 points and 3.2 rebounds per game as a sophomore and was a 38 percent 3-point shooter across two seasons at Duke.

Michigan won't have any of its championship frontcourt back, as projected first-round pick Morez Johnson Jr. will stay in the NBA Draft after a stellar season with the Wolverines. Johnson averaged 13.1 points, 7.3 rebounds and 1.1 blocks per game next to Yaxel Lendeborg and Aday Mara, starting all 40 games for the champs.

Texas forward Dailyn Swain said at the NBA Draft Combine that he wasn't giving any consideration to returning to school after one season with the Longhorns and three under Sean Miller. Swain, a projected late first-round pick, averaged 17.3 points, 7.5 rebounds and 3.6 assists per game last season, and he averaged 1.4 steals per game across three seasons.

North Carolina forward Henri Veesaar has been committed to staying in the draft throughout the process and reiterated at the combine that he "just felt like it was the right time to go to NBA." Veesaar was a centerpiece for the Tar Heels last season, averaging 17.0 points and 8.7 rebounds per game after transferring from Arizona, but North Carolina's coaching change played a role in shaking up the roster. Veesaar is considered a borderline first-round prospect.

title: Richey Stojakovic's stay-or-go choice simply the latest for Illini
description: May 27—CHAMPAIGN — The clock is ticking for Andrej Stojakovic. The deadline for early entrants in the 2026 NBA draft to either stay on that path or return to college basketball for another season hits at 10:59 p.m. Wednesday. It's a choice that will have ramifications for Stojakovic.
fullText:
May 27—CHAMPAIGN — The clock is ticking for Andrej Stojakovic.

The deadline for early entrants in the 2026 NBA draft to either stay on that path or return to college basketball for another season hits at 10:59 p.m. Wednesday.

It's a choice that will have ramifications for Stojakovic. A choice that seems to have a logical conclusion when all the pros and cons have been equally measured.

Stojakovic, in the best-case scenario, projects as a late second-round pick should he stay in the draft, which takes place June 23-24 in Brooklyn, N.Y. The latest mock from ESPN's Jeremy Woo has the 6-foot-7 Illinois guard slotted in as the No. 50 overall pick to the Toronto Raptors. Yahoo! Sports' Kevin O'Connor has Stojakovic landing with the Golden State Warriors as the 54th overall selection.

Should Stojakovic secure a two-way deal, he'd make just shy of $680,000 next season. Based on those specific mock draft scenarios, that two-way deal would likely mean spending the majority of the season in the G League with Raptors 905 or the Santa Cruz Warriors. The former plays in 5,000-seat Paramount Fine Foods Centre in Mississauga, Ontario. The latter's home arena — Kaiser Permanente Arena in Santa Cruz, Calif. — seats half that.

But it's an option. If Stojakovic is set on starting his professional career, there's probably a team that will use some of its second-round capital to make it happen.

It's not the best option, however, if logic wins the day.

Returning to Illinois would mean Stojakovic could probably make at least five times as much as that two-way deal with one last season in Champaign. That's the bottom-line going rate for a potential All-Big Ten caliber guard — especially one that just played a key role on a Final Four team.

Stojakovic would also get to play in front of regular sellout crowds both at State Farm Center and across the Big Ten and continue to work on his game (namely his three-point shooting) to improve his stock for a 2027 NBA draft that's considered weaker from a depth of prospects standpoint.

All worth considering with a stay-or-go draft decision to be made.

It's not the first time Illinois coach Brad Underwood has had to wait for the early entrant deadline to pass to know, for absolute certain, whether the roster he thinks he has will be the one he works with when eight weeks of summer workouts start in June.

No such waiting ensued a year ago with Kasparas Jakucionis and Will Riley both projected as first-round picks, but Underwood and the Illini staff have sweated out the withdrawal deadline before.

First with Ayo Dosunmu in 2020, who turned unfinished business into a No. 1 seed in the NCAA tournament for Illinois in his final season. Kofi Cockburn faced a similar choice after that 24-7 season — Big Ten tournament title included — and opted for another year in Champaign.

The pre-draft process in 2023 saw both Terrence Shannon Jr. and Coleman Hawkins take their respective decisions down to the deadline. Both chose to return to Illinois, and an Elite Eight run followed.

It's another indicator of how Underwood has re-established Illinois as one of the top programs in the country.

Six years passed between Dee Brown and James Augustine being selected in the second round of the 2006 NBA draft and Meyers Leonard becoming a lottery pick in 2012. The drought was longer between Leonard and Dosunmu winding up No. 38 overall to the Chicago Bulls in 2021.

Dosunmu didn't break the dam himself. It took a few more years, but Keaton Wagler's likely top 10 selection in less than a month will mark three consecutive drafts with an Illinois guard picked in the first round. Shannon to the Minnesota Timberwolves in 2024 and Jakucionis to the Miami Heat and Riley to the Washington Wizards in 2025.

Based on most projections, that would make Illinois, come late June, one of just three teams with at least one first-round pick in each of the last three NBA drafts.

Duke should follow Jared McCain in 2024 and Cooper Flagg, Kon Knueppel and Khaman Maluach in 2025 into Cameron Boozer (and maybe Isaiah Evans) next month. Baylor can follow up Ja'Kobe Walter and Yves Missi in 2024 and V.J. Edgecombe in 2025 with Cameron Carr (and maybe Tounde Yessoufou) in June. And if some NBA team falls in love with Alex Karaban or Tarris Reed Jr. enough to make them a late first-round pick in this year's draft, Connecticut would make it four programs with three consecutive drafts with a first-round pick.

That's elite company for Illinois and a far cry from the depths the program dipped at the end of Bruce Weber's tenure as coach into basically the entire John Groce era.

There have been marker signs of sustained success, of course, since Underwood took the job in March 2017. Particularly the past seven seasons that have included 167 wins, three Big Ten titles and six NCAA tournament appearances (seven if not for COVID) with the 2024 Elite Eight run and this past season's trip to the Final Four.

Success that Stojakovic could help extend if he decides to return to Illinois for the 2026-27 season. David Mirkovic, the Ivisic twins and Jake Davis are locked in to run it back. Stojakovic doing the same could make the Illini a national championship contender.

Then maybe he'll keep the streak going of first-round picks in 2027 with a fine-tuned jump shot and better decision-making in traffic. Unless five-star freshman Quentin Coleman beats him to it.
